head_coach.name: Dave Canales
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Pete Carroll / Seattle tree; physical run-first identity built around play-action and QB development; commitment to balance and heavy personnel groupings

offensive_coordinator.name: Brad Idzik
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Seattle Seahawks / Pete Carroll offensive tree via Dave Canales; West Coast concepts with physical run emphasis

defensive_coordinator.name: Ejiro Evero
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Vic Fangio 3-4 zone; mentored under Monte Kiffin (Tampa 2), Dom Capers (zone blitz), Wade Phillips, Raheem Morris (Rams); secondary/pass game coordinator pedigree

offense.base_personnel: 12 / 13 heavy; multiple TE sets featured prominently (13 personnel at 7.8%, 6th-highest in NFL in 2025)
offense.run_scheme: downhill inside zone / power; under-center heavy, physical run identity with commitment to establishing the ground game
offense.pass_concepts: play-action heavy off run-game foundation, boot concepts, quick game to protect QB, vertical shots off play-action; low-volume passing attack (bottom-third in passing yards per game)
offense.te_usage: versatile but primarily inline blocker / run-game contributor; TEs asked to do dirty work in run game and pass protection first, occasional routes; multiple TE sets (12/13 personnel) used heavily to establish physicality; TE receiving production historically low (last in NFL in TE targets over past 5 years)
offense.rb_usage: bell cow committee (Hubbard/Dowdle dual system); Hubbard valued for pass protection on third downs; run-first identity with moderate receiving involvement; primary runners with occasional checkdown targets

defense.alignment: odd front, 3 down linemen (NT, 2 DEs) with 2 stand-up OLBs; played base 3-4 at 4th-highest rate in NFL (31.1%); frequently shifts to nickel/sub packages
defense.edge_rush_source: OLB stand-up (Nic Scourton, Princely Umanmielen, DJ Wonnum, Patrick Jones II as stand-up OLBs); pass rush has been historically deficient under Evero — Panthers ranked last in pressure rate all three seasons
defense.coverage_base: Cover 3 heavy (ranked 2nd in NFL in Cover 3 usage); pre-snap two-high safety shell that rotates to single-high Cover 3 post-snap; zone dominant with heavy coverage disguises; bend-don't-break philosophy that caps explosive plays
defense.blitz_tendency: moderate; Evero more aggressive than Fangio tree norm — willing to bring 5-6 rushers when front four cannot generate pressure; safety blitzes and creative pressure packages used situationally
defense.lb_role: ILBs are run/coverage responsible; identified as biggest weakness on defense; need to be signal-callers and QB of the defense in Evero's zone scheme; coverage responsibility paramount
defense.safety_usage: extreme hybrid usage; Tre'von Moehrig deployed as chess piece across box (442 snaps), slot (310 snaps), free safety (143 snaps), and defensive line (94 snaps) in 2025; led all DBs in NFL in TFLs (14); used to disguise blitzes, rush passer (2.0 sacks, 12 QB pressures), cover slot, and play deep

te_receiving_boost.explanation: Canales has expressed strong affinity for TE usage and employs 12/13 personnel at elite rates (7.8% in 13 personnel, 6th-highest in NFL). However, TEs are primarily valued as inline blockers and run-game enablers first. Carolina ranked last in NFL in TE receiving production over the past five years. The offense asks TEs to 'do the dirty work' per Canales. A receiving TE would get moderate snaps but the scheme values blocking versatility over pure route-running. Sanders had only 29 catches for 190 yards in 2025. A TE who can block AND catch would be ideal, but pure receiving TEs will not see the volume they would in a spread/11-personnel offense.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Evero's 3-4 base defense makes OLBs the primary edge rushers. The Panthers have desperately needed pass-rush production from the OLB spot — they ranked dead last in pressure rate in all three seasons under Evero. Scourton and Umanmielen were 2025 draft picks specifically to rush from the stand-up OLB position. Any LB prospect with elite pass-rush traits (burst, bend, first step) would be immediately scheme-relevant as a stand-up edge rusher. The Panthers' biggest schematic need is pressure generation from the OLB spot to make Evero's zone coverage work.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: Carolina's offense is built around a physical, downhill running identity. Canales and Idzik committed to 'heavy personnel and under center, downhill runs.' Hubbard is valued on third downs primarily for pass protection, not receiving ability. The Panthers ranked bottom-third in passing yards and utilize RBs primarily as runners. Dowdle showed receiving ability but the offense does not create significant RB receiving volume by design. Pure runners with physicality are valued more than dual-threat backs.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: This is the defining positional value inflection for the Panthers defense. Tre'von Moehrig's usage in 2025 was the blueprint: 442 snaps in the box, 310 in the slot, 143 at free safety, 94 on the defensive line — one of only 11 defenders to log 50+ snaps at LOS, LB, slot CB, and safety. Evero explicitly called Moehrig 'a very, very, very unique chess piece' with 'the ability to play safety, linebacker, nickel, outside backer.' Moehrig led all NFL DBs in TFLs (14, most by a DB in a season in NFL history), had 2.0 sacks, 12 QB pressures (4th among safeties), and 28 run stops (5th among safeties). Any safety prospect with box versatility, tackling physicality, blitz ability, and slot coverage skills would be extraordinarily valuable to this defense.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: Carolina's two most actionable draft value inflections are at OLB/EDGE and hybrid safety. The 3-4 base under Evero makes stand-up OLB pass rushers the team's most urgent need — they have ranked last in pressure rate in all three Evero seasons, and the zone coverage scheme collapses without QB pressure. An LB with elite pass-rush traits (Arvell Reese, Sonny Styles types) should be valued at EDGE prices for CAR. Similarly, hybrid safeties who can play box, slot, and blitz are scheme-critical given Moehrig's deployment as the defense's most versatile weapon. On offense, the heavy personnel / run-first identity means pure receiving TEs get a moderate boost at best — CAR values blocking TEs who can also catch, not move TEs used as slot weapons. RB receiving value is low; CAR wants physical downhill runners. Brad Idzik takes over play-calling in 2026, but the offensive identity is expected to remain run-first and physical. Note: Panthers still need ILBs who can be signal-callers in Evero's zone scheme — coverage LBs with processing speed and communication skills are valued. The biggest draft value disconnect is that a player labeled 'LB' who is really a pass rusher or a player labeled 'S' who is really a box/slot hybrid will be valued significantly higher by CAR than their position label suggests.
